response:
## Results

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean validation accuracy and standard deviation over five random seeds for each configuration. Table 1 summarizes the performance of all variants, including the baseline, which corresponds to the method without the proposed component.

The baseline (variant_03) achieved a mean validation accuracy of 67.8% (±0.6), establishing the reference point for subsequent comparisons. Most variants incorporating the proposed component did not surpass this baseline. Specifically, variant_01 achieved 67.2% (±0.8), variant_02 66.9% (±0.7), variant_04 65.1% (±0.9), variant_05 66.3% (±1.0), variant_06 64.7% (±0.7), variant_07 65.8% (±0.8), variant_09 66.5% (±0.7), and variant_10 65.9% (±0.8). All of these variants underperformed relative to the baseline, with absolute accuracy differences ranging from –0.6 to –3.1 percentage points.

Notably, only variant_08 outperformed the baseline, achieving a validation accuracy of 70.4% (±0.5), which constitutes a substantial absolute gain of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline. This improvement is consistent across all random seeds, as reflected in the reduced standard deviation. These results indicate that, among all tested configurations, only variant_08 provides a clear empirical benefit, while the remaining variants either failed to improve or resulted in decreased performance.

In summary, the experimental results suggest that the effectiveness of the proposed component is highly sensitive to specific configuration choices. While most variants did not lead to improvements, one configuration (variant_08) demonstrated a significant and robust gain over the baseline, highlighting the importance of careful design and tuning in deploying the proposed method.
